result.chapter_summary: Chapter 1 argues that current reading tests are invalid because they attempt to measure general skills when they actually measure domain-specific knowledge and vocabulary. Hirsch contends that the long-term decline in American reading scores for seventeen-year-olds, despite gains in early-grade mechanics, is the direct result of testing regimens that ignore the slow-growth nature of the knowledge required for mature comprehension.

claims.text: The current outbreak of antitesting sentiment and student/parent boycotts of required tests is misguided.
claims.source_passage: This kind of activism seems misguided. Tests have always been necessary in education.

claims.text: The only way to make educational tests fair, productive, and fewer is to base them on well-defined, knowledge-based curriculums.
claims.source_passage: There’s just one way to do that—to base them on well-defined, knowledge-based curriculums. There is no other way of making tests fair and productive.

claims.text: The average reading score of graduating seventeen-year-olds is the single best measure of the academic quality of a school system.
claims.source_passage: If one had to choose a single measure of the academic quality of a school system, the average reading score of its graduating seventeen-year-olds would serve.

claims.text: Verbal scores at age seventeen predict college and career readiness as well as later economic success.
claims.source_passage: Verbal scores at that age predict students’ college and career readiness and their later economic success.

claims.text: A valid reading test is essentially a test of general knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: A reading test is a test of general knowledge and vocabulary; it gauges a student’s ability to operate effectively within the public sphere.

claims.text: A valid reading test probes a student's degree of initiation into the public sphere, which is a fundamental aim of education.
claims.source_passage: Since a valid reading test probes a student’s degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education

claims.text: Any policy that fails to improve reading test scores is a failed educational policy.
claims.source_passage: any policy that lowers or neglects to improve test scores in reading is a failed educational policy.

claims.text: American math scores for seventeen-year-olds have been stable for many years, while reading scores have declined.
claims.source_passage: In math, in contrast to reading, American scores for seventeen-year-olds have been stable for many years. While it’s disappointing that math scores at age seventeen haven’t improved markedly, at least they haven’t gone down, as reading has.

claims.text: Reading scores of seventeen-year-olds in 2012 were significantly lower than they were in 1988, prior to the enactment of No Child Left Behind.
claims.source_passage: For, in 2012, a decade after the test-intensive No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB) went into effect, the reading scores of seventeen-year-olds came in significantly lower than they had been in 1988 before NCLB was enacted.

claims.text: There is a significant anomaly where thirteen-year-olds make progress in reading but seventeen-year-olds remain flat or decline.
claims.source_passage: Thirteen-year-olds have made steady progress in reading, but the scores of seventeen-year-olds have remained flat.

claims.text: Intensive test preparation in early grades under high-stakes testing regimens does not improve mature reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Yet all that intensive test prep did not, in the end, help their mature reading.

claims.text: Testing regimens have successfully improved the mechanics of early reading, such as decoding fluency and accuracy.
claims.source_passage: the testing regimens have clearly helped improve the mechanics of early reading (that is, the ability to sound out fast and effortlessly)—an important gain. Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did before NCLB.

claims.text: Reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups narrow in early grades when fluency is the primary measure, but widen again between ages thirteen and seventeen.
claims.source_passage: Because of that the reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups have narrowed in those years, when fluency is an important part of the score. But, alas, those equity gaps begin widening again between ages thirteen and seventeen, when knowledge and vocabulary are decisive.

claims.text: Vocabulary size and knowledge are slow-growth plants determined by a child's overall experience from birth, not by high school instruction alone.
claims.source_passage: The vocabulary size of seventeen-year-olds is not determined at age seventeen, or sixteen, or fifteen. It’s a plant of slow growth that is determined by the knowledge that has been gradually acquired from a child’s overall experience from birth to age seventeen.

claims.text: The No Child Left Behind (NCLB) act led to an improvement in students' decoding skills and fluency in the early grades.
claims.source_passage: Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did before NCLB.

claims.text: Reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups have narrowed in the early years specifically because decoding/fluency is a primary component of those scores.
claims.source_passage: Because of that the reading gaps between ethnic and racial groups have narrowed in those years, when fluency is an important part of the score.

claims.text: Equity gaps in reading begin widening again between ages thirteen and seventeen because background knowledge and vocabulary become the decisive factors for comprehension.
claims.source_passage: But, alas, those equity gaps begin widening again between ages thirteen and seventeen, when knowledge and vocabulary are decisive.

claims.text: The vocabulary size of a seventeen-year-old is the result of gradual knowledge acquisition from birth rather than high school instruction.
claims.source_passage: The vocabulary size of seventeen-year-olds is not determined at age seventeen, or sixteen, or fifteen. It’s a plant of slow growth that is determined by the knowledge that has been gradually acquired from a child’s overall experience from birth to age seventeen.

claims.text: Current schooling in early grades has depressed the vocabulary growth of seventeen-year-olds by prioritizing test preparation over knowledge acquisition.
claims.source_passage: While current schooling in early grades has improved the sounding-out aspects of reading, we can infer from the NAEP results that it has depressed the vocabulary sizes of seventeen-year-olds.

claims.text: American public schooling declined in quality during the 1960s and 1970s.
claims.source_passage: That report showed that American public schooling had declined in quality during the 1960s and 1970s.

claims.text: Improvement in decoding only produces improvement in reading comprehension for undemanding texts on everyday topics.
claims.source_passage: That in turn should also have produced an improvement in reading comprehension, and it did so for undemanding texts on everyday topics in the early grades. But it has not produced improvement in the reading comprehension of more demanding texts on more demanding topics.

claims.text: High-stakes reading tests have forced schools to abandon the teaching of history and the arts in favor of 'reading' test preparation.
claims.source_passage: a number of schools told me, after high-stakes reading tests came into effect under NCLB, that they could no longer teach history and the arts. They now were being made to teach “reading” instead, with a strong emphasis on test preparation.

claims.text: Educational administrators incorrectly believe that formal strategy drills like 'making inferences' are more effective for raising scores than content learning.
claims.source_passage: Their higher-ups were under the impression that intense classes devoted to “making inferences” and “finding the main idea” would improve reading scores more effectively than learning about ancient Egypt or the solar system

claims.text: Common Core State Standards tests will likely depress future reading competence unless schools prioritize knowledge acquisition starting in early grades.
claims.source_passage: This chapter will predict that the high-stakes reading tests that accompany the new Common Core State Standards are going to have a similar nugatory or depressing effect on the reading competence of our seventeen-year-olds of the future—unless we take strong steps to make knowledge acquisition a chief goal of schooling starting in the earliest grades.

claims.text: Drills in formal comprehension skills, such as summarizing and questioning, provide an initial positive effect that quickly reaches a plateau.
claims.source_passage: And indeed students do show an initial positive effect from practicing finding the main idea. But their progress quickly reaches a limit and then halts.

claims.text: The time spent sitting for standardized tests is less damaging than the curricular distortion caused by the tests.
claims.source_passage: The school time usurped by sitting for the tests has not been the most disabling consequence of over-testing.

claims.text: Schooling has been made boring and ineffective by test-prep exercises in skills like 'summarizing' and 'questioning the author.'
claims.source_passage: In language arts, for instance, schooling has been bent out of shape and made boring and ineffective by test-prep exercises in skills like “summarizing” and “questioning the author.”

claims.text: Practice in finding the main idea shows an initial positive effect, but that progress quickly reaches a limit and halts.
claims.source_passage: And indeed students do show an initial positive effect from practicing finding the main idea. But their progress quickly reaches a limit and then halts.

claims.text: Drills in formal comprehension skills have not raised mature reading scores.
claims.source_passage: Drills in formal comprehension skills have not raised mature reading scores; rather, they have taken up a lot of class time that could have been devoted to knowledge building.

claims.text: The skill of reading comprehension in any given case depends more on relevant knowledge than on formal strategies.
claims.source_passage: We know that the skill of reading comprehension in any given case depends more on relevant knowledge than on formal strategies.

claims.text: Reading strategies, once briefly learned, will take care of themselves.
claims.source_passage: Once briefly learned, the strategies will take care of themselves.

claims.text: Focusing on knowledge building automatically creates better summarizers and main-idea finders.
claims.source_passage: The wise teacher and school will therefore create better summarizers and main-idea finders automatically if they focus on knowledge building—a happier, more productive, and far less boring focus for schooling.

claims.text: The unpredictability of topics on current reading tests forces schools to focus on strategies and skill drills rather than systematic knowledge acquisition.
claims.source_passage: The unpredictability of the test topics forces the schools to stress the externalities of test taking and of meaning guessing.

claims.text: Well-established reading tests like the Gates-MacGinitie and NAEP are technically reliable and valid measures of average reading ability.
claims.source_passage: Despite their indifference to the school curriculum, the better reading tests are technically reliable and valid. The Gates-MacGinitie reading test, the Iowa Test of Basic Skills, and the National Assessment of Educational Progress are good measures of the average reading abilities of large groups of students.

claims.text: Current reading tests are educationally invalid because they hinder rather than raise mature reading skills.
claims.source_passage: But these tests as currently used by the schools have hindered, not raised, mature reading skills. That is to say, they are technically valid but educationally invalid

claims.text: If a test actually hurts education, it is ultimately invalid for schooling, regardless of technical validity.
claims.source_passage: His key insight was that the technical validity of a test is of little value by itself. The point of educational testing is to help education. If a test actually hurts education, then it is ultimately invalid for schooling.

claims.text: Empty state standards promote the myth that there is a generalized main-idea-finding skill.
claims.source_passage: By focusing on main-idea finding, the standards promote the myth that there is a generalized main-idea-finding skill, which if practiced and developed will enhance reading ability.

claims.text: Reading test questions about main ideas and inference making are actually probing knowledge and vocabulary, not all-purpose strategies.
claims.source_passage: The test questions about main ideas and inference making imply the misleading message that they are probing all-purpose strategies and skills of predicting, summarizing, and “inferencing.” But they are doing no such thing. The tests are probing knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: Current standardized reading tests are consequentially invalid because they lead schools to engage in self-defeating practices.
claims.source_passage: to engage in self-defeating practices. They are consequentially invalid.

claims.text: The invalidity of reading tests stems from scientific shortcomings in the content-free state standards upon which the tests are based.
claims.source_passage: That defect lies less in the tests themselves than in the scientific shortcomings of the empty state standards on which they are based.

claims.text: Educational standards mislead schools into believing reading is a generalized skill rather than a knowledge-dependent activity.
claims.source_passage: The standards have misled the schools regarding the nature of reading skill.

claims.text: Current student performance on tests disproves the effectiveness of teaching generalized main-idea-finding skills.
claims.source_passage: But if that were so for mature reading ability, the current generation of students would be performing better on the tests, for they have all been well schooled in main-idea finding.

claims.text: Reading tests actually probe a student's background knowledge and vocabulary, not all-purpose strategies.
claims.source_passage: The tests are probing knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: The focus on decoding under the No Child Left Behind Act has been productive for early reading mechanics.
claims.source_passage: To the credit of the No Child Left Behind Act, the new focus on decoding has been highly productive for the mechanics of early reading.

claims.text: Relevant knowledge is the primary component of reading skill once a student has mastered decoding and fluency.
claims.source_passage: But once decoding has been mastered and fluency attained, relevant knowledge becomes the chief component of reading skill. Every cognitive scientist specializing in the subject would agree with that statement.

claims.text: Students with smaller relevant vocabularies and less background knowledge will perform worse on tests regardless of their training in formal reading skills.
claims.source_passage: no matter how well trained students become in main-idea finding, the student with the smaller relevant vocabulary and knowledge is the one who will fare worse on the test.

claims.text: The widespread protest and opting out of tests indicate that parents and teachers have correctly identified a fundamental failure in the current testing system.
claims.source_passage: That parents and teachers alike are demonstrating against the new tests, and opting out of them, is a pretty good indication that they have correctly concluded that something has gone wrong.

claims.text: Excessive test preparation has narrowed elementary education by marginalizing social studies, science, and the arts.
claims.source_passage: Teachers have complained that test preparation has narrowed elementary schooling—pushing out social studies, science, and the arts.

claims.text: Constant standardized testing causes significant and unproductive stress for students and their families.
claims.source_passage: Parents have complained of the neglect of history and the arts, adding that constant testing has placed unfruitful stress upon their children.

claims.text: Mathematics standards are superior to reading standards because they are based on specific content that builds progressively across grade levels.
claims.source_passage: Current math standards are much better guides for teachers and test makers than are current standards in reading.

claims.text: Standards makers avoid specifying reading content because they fear the political controversy associated with defining a national curriculum in literacy.
claims.source_passage: The makers of standards have decided that while it is politically feasible to create a definite content guide in math, fierce controversy would follow if they created a definite content guide in reading.

claims.text: Reading standards in states like Texas and Virginia are repetitive and contain no new intellectual content from grade to grade.
claims.source_passage: My own state of Virginia is more forthright about the inherent repetitiousness and content emptiness of its reading standard

claims.text: The Common Core State Standards in English language arts lack real progression in content from grade to grade.
claims.source_passage: Nor are the new Common Core State Standards in English language arts exempt from this same lack of real progression or content

claims.text: The belief that the skill of finding the main idea can substitute for specific content is actively misleading.
claims.source_passage: The notion that skill in finding the main idea can take the place of content is worse than empty; it’s actively misleading.

claims.text: There is no reliable, transferable 'main-idea-finding skill' independent of domain knowledge.
claims.source_passage: There is no reliable main-idea-finding skill. If readers understand a passage, they will reliably answer the test question about the main idea. If they don’t understand the passage, they won’t.

claims.text: Complex writing typically does not contain a single 'main idea.'
claims.source_passage: Moreover, in good, complex writing there isn’t usually a single main idea.

claims.text: Empty, skill-based standards were created primarily for political expediency.
claims.source_passage: These empty standards were created out of political expediency.

claims.text: Standards and test makers have built a fictional educational universe based on a faulty picture of reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The makers of standards and tests have built up an artificial construct, politically painless for the makers of standards and of tests, but based on a faulty and unproductive picture of reading comprehension.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not an all-purpose skill like decoding.
claims.source_passage: Test makers have dutifully followed the standards makers, presenting reading comprehension as an all-purpose skill like decoding. Since it isn’t, the current standards and tests have created a fictional alternative universe in our classrooms.

claims.text: Reading comprehension ability is dependent on passage-relevant knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: In the real world, an ability to comprehend a piece of writing depends on one’s having the knowledge and vocabulary relevant to that passage.

claims.text: Current standardized tests are unfair because they measure knowledge not explicitly taught by the school.
claims.source_passage: If the school does not teach students the knowledge and vocabulary they need to understand the passages on the test, then the test is unfair as a measure of what the school has successfully taught.

claims.text: Standardized tests reflect knowledge students gain from their overall environment rather than just school instruction.
claims.source_passage: The test simply reflects the knowledge and vocabulary that students have picked up from all sources.

claims.text: Current language arts standards do not specify the content that schools are supposed to teach.
claims.source_passage: Nor do the schools know what knowledge they are supposed to teach. The language arts standards do not specify content.

claims.text: Defining reading as a set of 'strategies' is a method used to make inherently unfair tests appear fair.
claims.source_passage: Under these circumstances, a method has had to be devised that seems to make these inherently unfair tests fair. That method has been to define reading ability as a set of strategies, and then to create test items that appear to probe those strategies.

claims.text: Test question formats (stems) misleadingly suggest they probe strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.

claims.text: Released items from Texas tests misleadingly indicate that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.
claims.source_passage: Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.

claims.text: Answering reading test items is easy for students who understand the passages but impossible for those who do not, regardless of strategy drill.
claims.source_passage: Answering such items is easy for students who understand the passages, but not for those who don’t—no matter how many drills in comprehension strategies the students had before the test.

claims.text: A child with relevant domain-specific background knowledge will understand a passage and find the right answer without conscious strategizing.
claims.source_passage: A child who has the relevant domain-specific background knowledge will understand the passage and get the right answer fast, without conscious strategizing.

claims.text: A fictitious educational world has been devised in which metaskills appear as important as knowledge and vocabulary to mask test unfairness.
claims.source_passage: In sum, to mask the inherent unfairness of these tests, a fictitious alternative world has been devised in which metaskills look as important as knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: Content-evading state standards defined the reading curriculum as a set of all-purpose techniques like inference making and predicting.
claims.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques like inference making, predicting, and main-idea finding.

claims.text: Reading strategy lessons offer an initial score boost but plateau quickly and do not require long-term practice.
claims.source_passage: Lessons in reading strategies offer an initial score boost for test taking, but are quickly learned; they plateau fast, and they don’t have to be practiced.

claims.text: The utility of reading comprehension strategies ceases after approximately ten lessons.
claims.source_passage: Their utility ceases after about ten lessons. Two weeks on comprehension strategies is optimal. There is no practical utility after that.

claims.text: Forcing young students to be self-conscious about applying reading strategies distracts them and degrades their actual performance.
claims.source_passage: Worse, making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

claims.text: The reading curriculum should be restructured to be like math, with specific content in standards and tests based on that content.
claims.source_passage: The structure will need to become more like that of math, with specific content in the standards and the curriculum, and with tests based on that content.

claims.text: General skills like 'close reading', 'complexity managing', and 'main-idea finding' do not actually exist as general skills.
claims.source_passage: the new versions of high-stakes reading tests that, as before, pretend to test the general skills of close reading, complexity managing, and main-idea finding—general skills that do not exist.

claims.text: The new reading tests based on the Common Core will not be more educationally productive than the tests they are displacing.
claims.source_passage: So, despite the expense and computerized novelty of the new reading tests, it will be hard for them to be any more educationally �productive 30  why knowledge matters than the tests they are displacing.

claims.text: The most important words in the Common Core standards are those calling for a curriculum intentionally structured to develop content knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Those precious words are to my mind the most important ones in the new standards.

claims.text: The Common Core standards for 'main-idea finding' and 'close reading' are content-free, strategy-like elements.
claims.source_passage: Those are the Common Core standards that are the most content-free, strategy-like elements.

claims.text: The ability to answer 'close-reading' and 'inference' test questions depends on a student's prior relevant knowledge of the words and topics, not hours of strategy practice.
claims.source_passage: the secret to answering such questions will not be hours of practice of ‘inferencing skills’ and ‘close-reading skills,’ but can only be answered through the student’s prior relevant knowledge of the words and the topics.

claims.text: Current 'close reading' test items often rely on a flawed theory of inference that assumes logical acumen can resolve lexical ambiguity.
claims.source_passage: It’s based on a wrong theory of inference and reading comprehension.

claims.text: Knowledge is the most promising avenue to improve reading skills for all students and narrow the achievement gap between demographic groups.
claims.source_passage: Knowledge is by far the most promising avenue to carry us out of the reading slump we are in. It is by far the most promising way to advance reading skill for all, and narrow the reading gap between demographic groups.

claims.text: Teachers feel compelled to engage in counter-productive test preparation because their job security is tied to test outcomes.
claims.source_passage: ‘Well, Mr. Hirsch, that’s all very well, but if you were in my shoes you’d realize that your job would be at stake if you did not do test preparation as instructed.’ Quite right.

claims.text: Gaining substantive knowledge is a superior long-range reading comprehension strategy compared to skill-based exercises.
claims.source_passage: When a student gains a real advance in substantive knowledge, it’s the best long-range comprehension strategy.

claims.text: Standardized tests can only be fair and productive if they are based on standards that provide specific guides to curricular content.
claims.source_passage: Only standards that are guides to curricular content can be foundations for fair and productive tests.

claims.text: Current strategy-based language arts exercises are knowledge-displacing and essentially useless after a few lessons.
claims.source_passage: replace exercises that are knowledge-displacing, soul-deadening, and essentially useless after ten lessons.

claims.text: Wasted hours in reading instruction should be replaced with subject matters that build knowledge and vocabulary to induce reading competence.
claims.source_passage: Those wasted hours ought to be spent on far more interesting and rewarding subject matters that will build up knowledge and vocabulary, and therefore induce greater reading competence.

claims.text: Reading exercises focused on skills rather than content are useless after ten lessons.
claims.source_passage: replace exercises that are knowledge-displacing, soul-deadening, and essentially useless after ten lessons.

claims.text: Standards must provide specific guides to curricular content to serve as a foundation for fair tests.
claims.source_passage: Only standards that are guides to curricular content can be foundations for fair and productive tests.

claims.text: Fair and productive accountability in reading is impossible without definite content standards.
claims.source_passage: But they must come to understand that without definite content standards, there can be no fair and productive accountability in reading.

claims.text: Authorities should specify grade-by-grade what children need to learn to reach long-range reading goals.
claims.source_passage: The authorities can’t just wave their hands and imperiously demand better reading without stating what children need to learn and schools need to teach in a given grade in order to gradually reach that long-range goal.

claims.text: Accountability advocates should collaborate with parents and teachers to define grade-by-grade knowledge requirements.
claims.source_passage: Accountability hawks... should join with parents and teachers to help define the grade-by-grade knowledge that all the children in their local purview should gain.

claims.text: Removing punitive threats from invalid reading tests is a necessary intermediate step for educational reform.
claims.source_passage: Meanwhile, a positive intermediate step would be to remove the punitive threats to teachers attached to our educationally invalid reading tests

claims.text: High-stakes testing prevents teachers from focusing on the long-term acquisition of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: If the high stakes were removed, sensible teachers and principals would be willing to pay more attention to the long arc of knowledge acquisition, which is the route to producing good readers and competent high school graduates.

claims.text: Recent reading tests make fraudulent claims when used to measure school effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: This chapter has shown that recent, technically valid reading tests make fraudulent claims if they are used to gauge what the schools have taught.

claims.text: Current reading tests have had a disastrously narrowing effect on American schooling.
claims.source_passage: And they have had a disastrously narrowing effect on schooling.

claims.text: Vocabulary size is the most accurate measure of the underlying competence required for reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Analyses of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showed that the vocabulary size test and the paragraph comprehension test correlate so well that they can be considered to represent the same underlying competence, which was most accurately measured by vocabulary size!

claims.text: Topic familiarity is a stronger predictor of reading comprehension than the ability to process difficult syntax.
claims.source_passage: This result anticipated recent work showing that topic familiarity, often indicated by word familiarity, is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with difficult syntax.

claims.text: Tests have always been a necessary component of education.
claims.source_passage: This kind of activism seems misguided. Tests have always been necessary in education.

claims.text: Initiating students into the public sphere is a fundamental aim of education.
claims.source_passage: Since a valid reading test probes a student’s degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education—any policy that lowers or neglects to improve test scores in reading is a failed educational policy.

claims.text: A technically valid reading test predicts a student's ability to comprehend both oral and written language.
claims.source_passage: A technically valid reading test that accurately predicts a student’s ability to comprehend diverse texts will also, self-evidently, predict that student’s ability to comprehend both oral and written language.

claims.text: The asterisks in NAEP long-term trend reports denote data analysis that goes deeper than surface-level averages.
claims.source_passage: Whether a prior score gets an asterisk is the result of data analysis that goes deeper than the surface averages.

claims.text: Performance at age thirteen is irrelevant to colleges, employers, and nations if verbal abilities decline by age seventeen.
claims.source_passage: No college, or employer, or nation much cares how well students did at age thirteen if by age seventeen their verbal abilities are worse than they were before the new reforms were instituted.

claims.text: Iowa was the only state that did not develop grade-by-grade reading standards following the education summits of 1989 and 1996.
claims.source_passage: After the governors’ education summits of 1989 and 1996, all but one state (Iowa) developed grade-by-grade reading standards on which schooling in the state would be based.

claims.text: Students in large American cities take approximately eight standardized tests per year, which accounts for over 4 percent of school time.
claims.source_passage: An alarming study recently produced by the Council of Great City Schools reporting that each student in the large cities now takes about eight standardized tests in a year. Just sitting for the tests takes over 4 percent of school time.

claims.text: A lack of relevant knowledge forces students to use test glosses and conscious strategy application, preventing them from finishing tests.
claims.source_passage: A child who does not have enough relevant knowledge will have to use special glosses in the test and consciously apply strategies; that child won’t finish the test and will get many answers wrong.

claims.text: The word 'disgorge' is linguistically inappropriate for describing violent, explosive actions.
claims.source_passage: I’d guess that the author meant to imply something not all that violent, since disgorge is usually not used to describe violent, explosive action.

claims.text: Accountability advocates have a responsibility to demonstrate grit by defining specific grade-by-grade knowledge requirements.
claims.source_passage: Accountability hawks need to show more grit in creating state and local curriculum standards. They should join with parents and teachers to help define the grade-by-grade knowledge that all the children in their local purview should gain.

evidence.description: NAEP 2012 Long-Term Trend report showing a statistically significant three-point drop in reading scores for 17-year-olds compared to 1988, 1990, and 1992.
evidence.source_reference: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) 2012 long-term trend report
evidence.source_passage: Figure 1.1 is taken from the 2012 long-term trend report of the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)... It shows a statistically significant drop of three points in the reading abilities of seventeen-year-olds, as compared with scores in 1988, 1990, and 1992.

evidence.description: Analysis of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showing that vocabulary size and paragraph comprehension tests correlate so highly they represent the same underlying competence.
evidence.source_reference: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen, Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data from the NLSY79 (2007)
evidence.source_passage: Analyses of the Armed Forces Qualification Test (AFQT) showed that the vocabulary size test and the paragraph comprehension test correlate so well that they can be considered to represent the same underlying competence, which was most accurately measured by vocabulary size!

evidence.description: Evidence showing that topic familiarity (often indicated by word familiarity) is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with syntax.
evidence.source_reference: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen (2007)
evidence.source_passage: This result anticipated recent work showing that topic familiarity, often indicated by word familiarity, is a stronger predictor of comprehension than formal ability to deal with difficult syntax.

evidence.description: NAEP (National Assessment of Educational Progress) results showing stagnant scores for seventeen-year-olds despite improvements in early-grade decoding.
The 1983 'A Nation at Risk' report documented a decline in the quality of American public schooling.
evidence.source_reference: National Commission on Excellence in Education, A Nation at Risk (1983)

evidence.description: A study by the Council of Great City Schools found that urban students take about eight standardized tests a year, consuming 4% of school time.
Metastudies and research reviews showing that formal strategy instruction quickly reaches a plateau in effectiveness.
evidence.source_reference: Rosenshine and Meister, 'Reciprocal Teaching' (1994); Willingham and Lovette (2014)

evidence.description: The U.S. Department of Education's 2015 testing action plan acknowledging that excessive testing can drain creativity and time from classrooms.
Metastudies on the limit and halt of progress from formal strategy instruction.
Correlation data for Gates-MacGinitie and Iowa tests showing high internal reliability (~.9) and correlation with other tests (~.7 to .8).
Comparison of student ability to read simple texts (Tyler Makes Pancakes!) versus mature texts (The Economic Consequences of the Peace).
The persistent low performance of students on reading tests despite widespread training in 'main-idea finding' strategies.
Scientific consensus among cognitive scientists regarding the role of knowledge in reading comprehension.
The growth of the 'opt-out' movement in various school districts.
New York Times report documenting student stress, medical intervention (Xanax), and teacher retirement due to testing culture.
Comparison of Texas Grade 3-5 Math standards (specific to fractions) vs. Texas Grade 3-8 Reading standards (repetitive focus on main idea).
Comparison of Virginia reading standards for Grades 2 through 8, showing the identical requirement to 'Identify the main idea' at every level.
Textual analysis of Common Core State Standards (Grades 3-8) showing a lack of content progression beyond repeating 'Determine the main/central idea'.
A thought experiment regarding the Pledge of Allegiance to demonstrate that complex texts have multiple main ideas.
Released test question 'stems' from Texas state tests (STAAR) which frame content-knowledge questions as strategy-based questions (e.g., 'The reader can infer...').
evidence.source_reference: Texas released test items for 5th and 7th grade

evidence.description: Released test items from Texas showing question stems focused on inference and main ideas.
evidence.source_reference: Texas released test items for fifth and seventh graders
evidence.source_passage: To keep Texas still in view, these stems were taken from released test questions in Texas for fifth and seventh graders.

evidence.description: A review article showing that reading strategy instruction has very limited and quickly plateauing benefits.
evidence.source_reference: Willingham and Lovette, 'Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?' (2014)
evidence.source_passage: The evidence is summarized in a recent review article by Willingham and Lovette, “Can Reading Comprehension Be Taught?” They answer: “Not really.”

evidence.description: Research showing that excessive self-consciousness about strategies can distract students and lower performance.
evidence.source_reference: Robert S. Siegler, 'Adaptive and Nonadaptive Characteristics of Low-Income Children’s Mathematical Strategy Use'
evidence.source_passage: Worse, making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

evidence.description: Sample test item from PARCC for grade 3 requiring students to define 'avenge' and provide supporting details from the text.
Sample test item from Smarter Balanced for grade 4 involving the word 'disgorging' in a sentence about nanodiamonds.
Reader comments from a Huffington Post article where teachers explain their professional necessity to follow test-prep mandates.
Research on the plateau of strategy instruction, suggesting children already possess basic comprehension strategies from oral speech.
evidence.source_reference: Rosenshine and Meister, 'Reciprocal Teaching: A Review of the Research' (1994)
evidence.source_passage: Rosenshine and Meister (“Reciprocal Teaching”) postulate that children already had to learn comprehension strategies in oral speech, and that the chief accomplishment of strategy instruction is to impart the realization that written speech is also somebody trying to tell you something.

concepts.author_definition: A test that measures a student's general knowledge and vocabulary, gauging their ability to operate effectively within the public sphere, rather than just measuring formal reading skills.
concepts.common_alternative: A test of transferable, general-purpose decoding and comprehension strategies.
concepts.source_passage: A reading test is a test of general knowledge and vocabulary; it gauges a student’s ability to operate effectively within the public sphere.

concepts.author_definition: The arena of civic and economic participation where success is predicted by the ability to comprehend communal oral and written language.
concepts.source_passage: gauges a student’s ability to operate effectively within the public sphere... a valid reading test probes a student’s degree of initiation into the public sphere—a fundamental aim of education.

concepts.author_definition: The foundational ability to sound out or decode words with speed, fluency, and accuracy.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes conflated with overall reading comprehension.
concepts.source_passage: the mechanics of early reading (that is, the ability to sound out fast and effortlessly)—an important gain. Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy

concepts.author_definition: The technical skill of turning written marks into sounds and words, or 'sounding out.'

concepts.term: Vocabulary as a 'plant of slow growth'
concepts.author_definition: The concept that vocabulary size is not a skill to be drilled but a cumulative result of long-term knowledge acquisition across all experiences.
Instructional strategies like 'finding the main idea,' 'summarizing,' or 'making inferences' that are taught as general, transferable skills.
A test's validity based on its ultimate effect on education; if a test's use harms the educational process, it lacks consequential validity.
concepts.common_alternative: Commonly tests are judged only by technical reliability and statistical validity.

concepts.author_definition: Curricular standards that focus on formal skills like 'finding the main idea' rather than specifying substantive subject-matter knowledge.
Superficial skills such as strategy drills and meaning guessing forced upon schools by the structure of unpredictable standardized tests.
A defect in testing where the test itself or the standards it is based on lead to educational practices that are counterproductive to the test's stated goals.
The failure of standards makers to specify concrete topics or texts in reading due to fear of political controversy.
A mythical, all-purpose strategy that supposedly allows a student to understand any text regardless of their background knowledge.
A school test that measures knowledge or vocabulary that was not explicitly taught within the school's curriculum, thereby reflecting outside privileges rather than instructional success.
Cognitive strategies like 'inference making' or 'finding the main idea' that are falsely treated as transferable skills independent of subject-matter knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: General cognitive abilities that can be improved through drill and applied to any text.
concepts.source_passage: The substance of the reading curriculum had been described in the content-evading state standards as consisting of all-purpose techniques like inference making, predicting, and main-idea finding.

concepts.author_definition: In the context of Common Core, a supposedly new strategy that the author views as another content-free metaskill that masks the necessity of background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A rigorous technique for analyzing the internal mechanics and meaning of a text.
concepts.source_passage: The new test items will need to probe main-idea-finding skills and inference-making skills, as before, and now they will probe close-reading skills as well.

concepts.author_definition: A process the author argues is incorrectly modeled by testers as a deductive-syllogistic process, whereas in reading, it actually depends on background knowledge of the situation model.

A term for tests that may be technically accurate in what they measure (e.g., vocabulary) but are invalid because their use leads to harmful educational outcomes, such as narrowing the curriculum to useless skills.
concepts.common_alternative: Usually validity refers only to whether a test measures what it claims to measure.
concepts.source_passage: They are “consequentially invalid.” They do more harm than good.

concepts.author_definition: Reading strategy exercises and drills that consume instructional time that should instead be used to build substantive subject-matter knowledge.
The process of a student gaining the general knowledge and vocabulary necessary to operate effectively within communal discourse.
A self-defeating educational practice forced on schools by the unpredictability of test topics on content-free reading assessments.
The phenomenon where misguided efforts to raise reading scores (through test-prep) actually resulted in the long-term depression of those scores.
Policy makers and advocates who emphasize high-stakes testing and standards-based reform to ensure educational progress.

cases.name: The United States under No Child Left Behind (NCLB)
cases.description: A decade-long national educational period starting in 2002 characterized by high-stakes testing. The author notes that while this era improved early decoding and narrowed early achievement gaps, it ultimately failed to improve reading comprehension for seventeen-year-olds, whose scores actually dropped compared to the pre-NCLB era.
key_passages: In 2012, a decade after the test-intensive No Child Left Behind Act (NCLB) went into effect, the reading scores of seventeen-year-olds came in significantly lower than they had been in 1988 before NCLB was enacted.
They would have experienced almost ten years of high-stakes testing in reading under NCLB... Yet all that intensive test prep did not, in the end, help their mature reading.
Younger students can now decode texts with more fluency and accuracy than they did before NCLB... but, alas, those equity gaps begin widening again between ages thirteen and seventeen.
contestable_aspects: Attribution of the score decline specifically to the testing regimens rather than other social or technological factors.
The characterization of NCLB as purely 'test-intensive' without credit for other pedagogical shifts.

cases.name: Texas State Standards
cases.description: Hirsch compares the Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS) for math and reading. He highlights that math standards for grades 3-5 provide specific, escalating content regarding fractions, while the reading standards for grades 3-8 merely repeat the requirement to 'summarize main ideas' without adding new content or complexity.
key_passages: I’ll illustrate this with a few examples comparing the two kinds of standards. Here are some current Texas math standards concerning fractions.

Here are some Texas reading standards concerning informational texts. They are neither specific nor progressive. Wary of specifying either topics or texts, the standards makers focus on the skill of finding the main idea.

cases.name: No Child Left Behind (NCLB) decoding focus
cases.description: Hirsch acknowledges NCLB's success in improving the mechanics of early reading (decoding and fluency), using it as a starting point to argue that knowledge becomes the primary factor once these mechanics are mastered.
key_passages: To the credit of the No Child Left Behind Act, the new focus on decoding has been highly productive for the mechanics of early reading.

cases.name: Texas and Virginia State Testing
cases.description: Hirsch uses these states as examples of testing systems that did not adopt Common Core but still rely on the 'strategy-based' testing model that emphasizes inference over content.
key_passages: I offer examples from Texas and Virginia, since those states did not sign on to the Common Core, and the standards are in force.
Their form indicates misleadingly that strategy expertise rather than specific knowledge is being probed.

cases.name: Common Core Reading Tests
cases.description: The author examines the new generation of tests, arguing that despite better rhetoric in the standards themselves, the tests remain focused on content-free skills like 'complexity managing' and 'close reading'.
key_passages: the makers of the Common Core tests are placed in the same position as the makers of the previous tests... The new test items will need to probe main-idea-finding skills and inference-making skills, as before, and now they will probe close-reading skills as well.

cases.name: PARCC (Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers)
cases.description: One of two major test consortia for the Common Core, which developed two-part questions to gauge comprehension and justification through 'close reading.'
key_passages: Thus PARCC, one of the two test consortia, has developed two-part questions, the first part gauging comprehension of a passage, the second part demanding a close reading and ‘logical inference’ to justify the student’s answer.

cases.name: Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium
cases.description: A test consortium that uses single questions to test word meaning based on context, which Hirsch uses to show that such questions are often ambiguous or knowledge-dependent.
key_passages: The ‘Smarter Balanced’ consortium puts the two-part structure into a single question, such as this one for grade 4

thinkers.name: Pamela Ing, Carole A. Lunney, and Randall J. Olsen
thinkers.key_idea: Vocabulary size and paragraph comprehension measure the same underlying factor, making vocabulary size an efficient proxy for overall reading competence.
thinkers.source_work: Reanalysis of the 1980 AFQT Data from the NLSY79 (2007)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their statistical reanalysis of military testing data to justify his central claim that reading comprehension is actually a function of vocabulary and general knowledge.
key_passages: The apparent fact that the two tests [vocabulary and comprehension] measure the same underlying factor suggests an efficient alternative for computing an AFQT is to simply drop the difficult-to-administer paragraph comprehension items and focus on the word-knowledge test.

thinkers.name: Arne Duncan and Barack Obama
thinkers.key_idea: Standardized testing has become excessive and can negatively impact the classroom environment if done poorly.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their recent (2015) self-criticism of testing regimes to validate his claim that testing has become a 'disabling consequence' for schools.
key_passages: the too-frequent testing of students in American schools has at last become a subject of concern and even self-criticism by the US Secretary of Education and the President of the United States.

thinkers.name: Barak Rosenshine and Carla Meister
thinkers.key_idea: Formal comprehension strategies (like reciprocal teaching) show a quick plateau in effectiveness.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their review to support his claim that drills in comprehension skills do not lead to continuous improvement in reading.
key_passages: Drills in formal comprehension skills have not raised mature reading scores... We know this from various metastudies.

thinkers.name: Samuel Messick
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Messick's theory to distinguish between tests that are statistically accurate ('technically valid') but harmful to school practice ('educationally invalid').
key_passages: His phrase was “consequential validity.” His key insight was that the technical validity of a test is of little value by itself.

thinkers.name: Cognitive Scientists
thinkers.key_idea: Relevant knowledge is the chief component of reading comprehension once decoding is mastered.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses the universal agreement of this group to validate his core claim that knowledge, not 'skills,' drives reading ability.
key_passages: Every cognitive scientist specializing in the subject would agree with that statement.

thinkers.name: Daniel T. Willingham and Gail Lovette
thinkers.key_idea: Reading comprehension strategies offer only a temporary boost and are not a substitute for knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their review of research as the definitive evidence for the limited utility of strategy instruction.
key_passages: They answer: “Not really.” Lessons in reading strategies offer an initial score boost for test taking, but are quickly learned; they plateau fast, and they don’t have to be practiced.

thinkers.name: Robert S. Siegler
thinkers.key_idea: Self-conscious strategy use can interfere with performance in low-income children.
thinkers.source_work: Adaptive and Nonadaptive Characteristics of Low-Income Children’s Mathematical Strategy Use
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Siegler to argue that the 'strategy-heavy' approach to reading might actually be harming the very students it is meant to help.
key_passages: making young students become highly self-conscious about applying strategies distracts their attention and degrades their performance.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: Source of the long-term trend data used to identify the decline in seventeen-year-old reading scores.

actors.name: National Center for Education Statistics
actors.context: Source agency for the NAEP data cited in Figure 1.1.

actors.name: Council of Great City Schools
actors.context: Cited for their study on the frequency of testing in urban school districts.

actors.name: US Department of Education
actors.context: Cited for its 2015 report criticizing excessive and low-quality testing.

actors.name: Superintendents and Principals
actors.context: Described as 'nervous' and acting on the false theory that strategies are the keys to reading comprehension.

actors.name: National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP)
actors.context: Cited as a reliable measure of average reading ability that reveals the stagnation of older students.

actors.name: Dawn LaBorde
actors.context: A parent from Palm Beach County cited in a New York Times story regarding the emotional stress testing puts on children.

actors.name: Texas Standards Makers
actors.context: The authors of the state standards who chose to avoid content in reading while including it in math.

actors.name: Makers of standards and tests
actors.context: Accused of creating artificial constructs and empty standards for political expediency.

actors.name: Common Core State Standards
actors.context: Criticized for lacking content progression and sending false messages about general skills.

actors.name: Texas Education Agency
actors.context: Cited as the source of test items that Hirsch critiques for focusing on metaskills.

actors.name: Common Core State Standards Initiative
actors.context: Acknowledged for having good intentions regarding content but criticized for its testing implementations.

actors.name: PARCC
actors.context: One of the test consortia creating Common Core-aligned reading assessments.

actors.name: Smarter Balanced
actors.context: The second major consortium creating Common Core-aligned reading assessments.

actors.name: American Teachers
actors.context: Respondents to Hirsch's article who express being trapped by accountability measures into doing test-prep instead of knowledge-building.

actors.name: Accountability Hawks
actors.context: Policy makers who demand testing and accountability but fail to specify the content curriculum that makes such accountability fair.

actors.name: Parents
actors.context: Encouraged to demand knowledge-building substance in classrooms and join the effort to define local curriculum standards.

objections_raised.objection: Testing is inherently bad and should be boycotted by parents and students.
objections_raised.response: Tests are necessary for education; the solution is not to eliminate them but to make them fewer and better by basing them on a specific knowledge-based curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: High schools are responsible for the decline in reading scores between age thirteen and age seventeen.
objections_raised.source: Unattributed (described as an inclination of observers)
objections_raised.response: Vocabulary and knowledge are 'plants of slow growth' accumulated from birth; the deficit is a cumulative result of early schooling and experience, not a failure of the high school years themselves.

objections_raised.objection: Standardized tests must use unpredictable topics to be fair when the curriculum is unknown.
objections_raised.response: This unpredictability forces schools to ignore knowledge building and focus on self-defeating strategy drills.

objections_raised.objection: Reading comprehension can be improved by teaching generalized main-idea-finding skills.
objections_raised.response: If this were true, students who are heavily drilled in these skills would show better performance on mature reading tests, which they do not.

objections_raised.objection: Test makers argue they cannot create knowledge-based tests because the state standards they must follow are content-free.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch argues that standards makers are engaged in a 'content cop-out' due to political fear, whereas math standards prove that specific content guides are possible and effective.

objections_raised.objection: The creation of skill-based standards and tests was a cynical or malicious act by educators.
objections_raised.response: It wasn't necessarily cynical; it was 'simply the best that could be done in the absence of a curriculum.'

objections_raised.objection: Don't all-purpose reading strategies actually exist, and won't learning them improve reading performance?
objections_raised.response: They exist, but their utility is very limited; they are learned quickly (in about two weeks) and do not benefit from the massive amounts of practice time currently allocated to them in schools.

objections_raised.objection: Teaching broad knowledge like history and science is fine in theory, but teachers' jobs are at stake if they don't follow specific test-prep instructions.
objections_raised.source: Teachers responding to Hirsch's Huffington Post piece
objections_raised.response: Hirsch concedes that teachers are in a difficult bind and that parents must get involved to demand knowledge-building substance to replace soul-deadening test-prep.

objections_raised.objection: Inferences can be made through 'close reading' and 'logical acumen' alone by looking at textual clues like word explosions.
objections_raised.source: Implicit in Common Core/Test Consortia design
objections_raised.response: In many cases, multiple answers can be logically justified unless the reader has specific background knowledge; words are ambiguous and depend on situational context, not just syllogistic logic.

objections_raised.objection: We need tests and schools must be held accountable for results.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch agrees that we need tests and accountability, but argues that accountability is only fair and productive if it is based on definite content standards rather than general skills.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
a subject I take up in more detail in the next chapter
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
the subject of the next chapter
This point is amplified with references to research in chapter 6.
The defects of the close-reading standard are discussed in chapter 6, on the Common Core Standards.
